title: AI Could Change Education Forever – Neeru Khosla Explains Why

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Today I'm speaking with Michael Nielsen. You have done many things. You were one of the pioneers of quantum computing, wrote the main textbook in the field, of the open science movement. You wrote a book about deep learning that Chris Olah and Greg Brockman credit it with getting them into the field. Um, more recently, you're a research fellow at the Astera Institute and writing a book about religion, science, and technology. I'm going to ask you about none of those things. The conversation I want to have today is: how do we recognize scientific progress? And it's especially relevant, uh, for AI, because people are trying to close the RL verification loop on scientific discovery, and what does it mean to close that loop? But in preparing for this interview, I've realized that it's a more mysterious and elusive force even in the history of human science than I understood. And I think a good place to start will be Michelson-Morley and how special relativity is discovered, if it's different than the story that you kind of get off of YouTube videos. Um, anyways, I will prompt you that way and then we'll go from there.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Okay, yes. So, Michelson-Morley is, uh, one of the sort of the famous results often presented as, as this experiment that was done in the 1880s and that helped Einstein, you know, come up with the special theory of relativity a little bit later, so sort of changing out the the way we think about space and and time and our fundamental conception of those things. And there's kind of a a big gap, I think, between the way Michelson and Morley and other people at the time thought about the experiment and certainly the way in which Einstein thought or did not think about the experiment. In actual fact, he stated later in his life he wasn't even sure whether he was aware of the paper at the time. There's a lot of evidence that he probably was aware of the paper at the time, but it actually wasn't dispositive for his thinking at all. Something else completely was was going on. So what Michelson and Morley thought they were doing was they thought they were testing different theories of what was called the ether. So if you go back to the 1600s, Robert Boyle introduced the idea of the ether and basically the idea of the ether is, you know, we know that sound is vibrations in the air. And then Boyle and other people got interested in the question of like is is light vibrations in something? And they couldn't figure out what it was. Boyle actually did an experiment where he he tested whether or not you could propagate light through a vacuum. He found that you could, you couldn't do it with with sound. So he introduced this idea of the ether. And then for the next 200 or so years, people had all these kind of conversations about about what the ether was and what its nature was. And the Michelson and Morley experiment was really an experiment to test different theories of the ether against one another and in particular to find out whether or not there was a so-called ether wind. So the idea was that the the Earth is passing through maybe this ether wind and if it is passing through the ether wind, sort of this background...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And you shoot a light beam sort of parallel to the direction the ether wind is going in, it'll get accelerated a little bit, and if it's being passed back sort of in the opposite direction, it'll get slowed down a little bit. And you should be able to see this in the results of interference experiments. And what they found, much to their surprise, I think, was that in fact there was no ether wind, and that ruled out some theories of the ether, but not all. And Michelson certainly continued to believe in the ether.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Okay, so this is what was a shocking part of reading this story from the biography of Einstein that you recommended by—what was his first name?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Abraham Pais.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Subtle is the Lord. And also from Imre Lakatos, 'The Methodologies of Scientific Research Programmes'. The way it's told is that Michelson-Morley proved that the ether did not exist, therefore it created a crisis in physics that Einstein solved with special relativity. And what you're pointing out is actually he was trying to distinguish between many different theories of ether, you know, if you're in space or if you're on Earth, it's the same direction of ether, or maybe the ether wind is being carried around by the Earth and so you can't really experience it on Earth, but if you go to high enough altitude, you might be able to experience it. In fact, the Michelson's experiments, the famous one is 1887, but he conducted these experiments for basically two decades.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I mean, for longer than that. He conducted them, I think the first one was in 1881, but he continued to believe until—I mean, he died, he died, I think it was like 1929 or so, it was like the late '20s. And he was still doing experiments in the 1920s, sort of about whether or not, you know, the ether existed. And so, so he continued to believe in the ether to the end of his life, or I think the last public statement he made is like a year or two before he died and he still basically believed at that point.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: And in fact, there was another physicist, Miller, who kept doing his experiments and in the 1920s he thought that he went to a high enough altitude, in Mount Wilson in California, where I'm high enough that I can actually—the ether winds are not being dragged with it by the Earth and I've measured the effect of the ether. And Einstein hears about this and he says—this is where you get the famous quote—'Subtle is the Lord but malicious he is not.' Anyways, I think the reason the story is interesting is for many different reasons, but one is one of the different ways in which the real history of science is different from this idea you get of the scientific method is you really can't apply falsification as easily as you might think. It's not clear what is being falsified. Is it just another version of the theory of the ether that's being falsified or, certainly you can't induce the theory of special relativity from the fact that one version of the ether seems to be disconfirmed by these experiments.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I mean, certainly doesn't show that, you know, ideas about falsification are wrong, or falsified. But it does show that sort of the most naive ideas, you know, are, things are much, often much more complicated than you think. So Michelson did this experiment in 1881, he was a very young man and then other people, I think Rayleigh was one of them.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: pointed out that there was some problems with the way you did it, so they had to redo it in 19- in 1887, and at that point like a lot of the leading physicists of the day, leading scientists of the day, basically accepted this result that there was no ether wind. But what- what to do about this? So yeah sure maybe you've falsified some theories of the ether, there are others that you haven't falsified at all at this point and and people sort of set to work on developing those. I mean it is funny I mean people will phrase it as showed that there was you know that the ether didn't exist and even just the word the there is kind of a misnomer. You know you actually had a ton of different different theories and a couple of leading contenders. So yeah there's some version of falsification going on but like how how you respond to this new experiment is very, very complicated. And- and most people responded, I mean certainly the leading physicists of the day responded by by saying okay, this gives us a lot of information about what the ether must be but it doesn't tell us that there is no ether.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: In fact, Lorentz at the end of the 19th century before Einstein figures out the math how you convert from one reference frame to another reference frame, comes up with the Lorentz transformations which is basically the basis of special relativity. But his interpretation is that you are converting from the ether reference frame to these non-privileged other reference frames if you're moving relative to the ether. And his interpretation of length contraction and time dilation is that this is the effect of moving through the ether and you have this pressure and that pressure is warping clocks, it's warping measures of length. And the interesting thing here is that experimentally you cannot distinguish Lorentz's interpretation from special relativity.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think that's a strong statement. I mean Lorentz introduces this quantity called local time, which he regards as- he's not trying- my understanding is he's not trying to give a really a physical interpretation of this, but it's what Einstein would later just recognize as time in a- in another inertial reference frame. And he's not trying to attribute much physical meaning to it. I think Poincare gets much closer to later on to realizing that no actually this is the time that's registered by- by clocks. But if you think about, you go what is it, it's 40 odd years later, people start doing these muon experiments where they see basically cosmic rays hit the top of the atmosphere, they produce a shower of muons and you can look to see at different heights in the atmosphere, you can look to see how many of those muons remain, and they decay over time. And a very strange thing happens, which is that they're decaying way, way, way too slow. So you sort of expect, actually they shouldn't really- they

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Couldn't be able to last the whole way through the atmosphere at all, there's just uh, their decay, their decay rate is is is too quick. Um, if if you were in a classical theory.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: but if in fact their time really has slowed down, it's okay,

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: and in fact you know the measured decay rates in in uh 1940 and then there have since been more accurate experiments done match exactly what you expect from special relativity. Um so so you know that's the kind of thing where again if Lorentz had been alive, he he'd been dead uh 10 or so years at that point. If he'd been alive you know I'm sure he would have tried or it seems quite likely that he would have tried to save his theory by patching it up yet again. But but it would have been a massive I mean that's a real setback. It starts to just look like oh no time is uh you know this thing that Lorentz introduced as a mathematical convenience. No no no that's actually what time is.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Right. For for the muons at least and then you know there's a whole bunch of other experiments that show this very similar phenomenon. And when was that experiment done? That was I think 1940 or 19, it might have been published in 1941. So maybe to then rephrase, uh change my claim, um, it's not that you couldn't distinguish them, but the scientific community adopted what we in retrospect consider the more correct interpretation before it was actually empirically or experimentally um shown to be preferred. So there's clearly some process that human science does which can distinguish different theories.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, can I just interrupt? I mean you used the word process and it's sort of it's interesting to think about about that that term. Like process kind of carries connotations of of you know it's something set in advance, it's something um and it's it's much more complicated in in practice. You you have people like like Lorentz who I mean Einstein just just absolutely utterly admired um and and Poincaré, one of you know the greatest scientists who ever lived um uh and Michaelson, I mean another truly outstanding scientist, never reconciled themselves. So it's not as though there's like some standard procedure that we're all using to like reconcile these things. No, like great scientists can remain long very can remain wrong for a very long time after the scientific community has broadly changed its its opinion. But there's no there's no centralized authority right sort of saying or centralized method.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I mean that is the interesting thing, that like there's progress even though it is hard to articulate the process by which it happens. The heuristics that are used. Anyways, you mentioned Poincaré and so Lorentz has the math right but the interpretation wrong and you should explain. It seems like Poincaré had the opposite where he understood that it's hard to define simultaneity because it requires some circular definition with time um or velocity of something that might be you know arrive at a midpoint together but velocity is defined in terms of time. Um and I find this interesting, there's a couple other examples we could call on but like there is a...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: phenomenon in the history of science or somebody asked the right question, um, but then they don't sort of clinch it. And I'm curious what you think is happening in those cases.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I mean, uh, I think you sort of you actually do want to go case by case and try and understand, it's not necessarily clear that they're doing the same thing wrong in all of the cases. I mean, the Poincaré case is, is amazing. Uh, he seems to have understood the principle of relativity, the idea that, that the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames. He seems to have understood that the speed of light is the same in all inertial reference frames. He, he doesn't actually phrase it quite that way, uh, but but is my understanding, but I don't speak French but, um, uh, you know and this is, I mean these are basic, these are the ideas that Einstein uses to deduce special relativity. But then he also has this additional sort of misunderstanding where he thinks, uh, that length contraction is a dynamical effect that somehow, um, you know sort of particles are being pushed together by by you know some external force, some something is going on dynamically. And he doesn't understand that it's purely kinematics, that actually space and time are different than than what we thought and you need to fundamentally rethink those those things. So it's almost like he almost like he knew too much. Uh, you know he had a sort of almost too grand of a vision in mind and Einstein sort of almost subtracts from that and and says no, no, no, it's space and time are just different than than what we thought. Um, um, and and you know here's the correct picture and there's a paper in I think it's 1909 where Poincaré like he's still got this dynamical picture of what's going on with the length contraction and we just, you know this is just not necessary, this is this is a mistake, um, from the modern point of view. And so why why is he doing this? Like why is he clinging on to this idea? And I don't know, I've you know obviously never met the man, uh, it it it would be fascinating to be able to to talk it over and to try and understand. But you know his expertise seems to be getting in the way. He knows so much, he understands so much, um, and then he's not able to let go of these these things. Actually, a really interesting fact, um, is that a few years prior, so 1890s Einstein's a teenager, he believes in the ether too. Like he knows about this stuff but like he's just not quite as attached obviously as as these older older people were and and maybe they were a little bit prisoner of their own expertise. That's that's my guess, I mean historians of science could could might would certainly disagree.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Well, there's then there's the obvious stories where Einstein himself later on is said to have not latched on to the correct interpretations of, uh, quantum mechanics or cosmology because of his own attachments.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think that the bigger question I have is like-

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: curriculum. And so maybe it's a mistake for not having done that or also like there's no real way to prep for Terence Tao or something and like um there's no curriculum that's like a plausible one. I think um well there's one failure mode... so there's many failure modes, but one is um if you you could have one dynamic I'm worried about, a long-term dynamic, is that you do good po- you can have a good podcast and this is a local maximum but um for no particular guest or topic are you going deep enough that you- I think my model of learning is there's no- if you don't really understand the deeper mechanism, you're just mapping inputs and outputs of a black box.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And that just fades incredibly fast or is not worth it in the first place and you kind of just move on and it's over. Um and you kind of need to build the intermediate connection. Um and it's unclear- I think actually AI in a weird way is really easy for that reason because there is a clear thing you can do, just implement it, right? And then you understand that. If I applied that criterion elsewhere, what am I- how do I just not do history episodes? Ada Palmer-

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Exactly, Ada Palmer. Like what what- wonderful to talk to, incredibly interesting, but for you personally like what changed?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there's some things I learned. I think I could have done a- if I had maybe allocated more time especially after the interview to like write up 2,000 words on everything I learned and how it connects to other things I know and something. Um and maybe that's the thing worth doing is spreading out the episodes more and spending more time afterwards consolidating.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But yeah, I think the- I would pay basically infinite amounts of money if there's somebody who's really good at coming up with: here is- here's the curriculum and here's the practice problems you need to do and here's the exercises you need to do after the interview to clamp what you have learned.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Have you tried doing that with somebody?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It's hard to find some- I mean I haven't tried super hard to, but um it seems ex- it seems it would be tough to find somebody who could do that for every single kind of discipline. Maybe I should just hire different ones for different topics.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Maybe. Or there's something real- like I mean what problem you know are you solving sort of for each episode? And I mean as far as I can tell like that's the only way I really understand anything is that you know I get interested in something. At first I don't even have a problem, but there's just some sense of there's some contribution to make here and gradually you hone in and oh there's a problem. And then you- I mean funnily enough, I mean spending time stuck is incredibly important um and and I sort of you know that used to just be annoying, now it seems like oh this is actually um uh maybe even the most important part of the whole process um that that very hard-won-ness of it means that you know I internalize it afterwards. I often find actually if I you know I've written sometimes 10,000 word essays in you know a couple of days and I've written them in you know three months or six months. I feel like I didn't learn very much from the ones that that only took a couple of days.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I understand.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Whereas, you know, some other ones that took three months, I'll be, you know, 15 years later, I'll, I'll still remember.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, physics came outside of physics, how you learn of the ones that took three months.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I mean, by far the most, you know, the common things, there's always some creative artifact. Sometimes it's a class, sometimes it's engagement with a group of people who, you know, there's some collective creative artifact that you're working on together. I mean, you might not even be aware of it, but you're acting as an input to their creative ends in some way. And sometimes it's just, you know, it's an essay or a book or whatever. It's one of the reasons why I often quite enjoy doing podcasts. I mean, particularly, I mean, I said yes to come here partially because I know you ask unusually demanding questions and so it's sort of that's an attempt to get this sort of perspective from a different, it's a different kind of a forcing function. So yeah, trying to pick sort of the most demanding creative context.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so for this interview I went through like three lectures of the Susskind theoretical minimum book. And the problem is that there's almost no practice problems in it. And so I hired a physics friend who's going to like, I haven't done it yet, but it's like every lecture I want like a bunch of practice problems to go through them and I'm planning on being appropriately humbled.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: But how do you make it as jugular as possible, right? Like, the higher you can raise the stakes, the better. I mean the interview is in some sense high stakes, but also it doesn't necessarily test deep understanding.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but I don't think the interview is that high stakes, right? You're not writing a book about special relativity and you're not trying to write a book that replaces the current, whatever the existing standard textbook is. Like that's a really high, really high stake.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: One of my, a phrase that I sort of find particularly difficult and... it's a funny one. People will talk about going deep on a subject and it turns out, you know, different people have different ideas of what this means. Some people mean they read a couple of blog posts, some people it means they read a book about it, some people it means they wrote a book about it. And I think like sort of what your standard is, the sort of the standard you hold yourself to determines a lot about your ability to integrate knowledge in this way.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I don't know what your experience has been, but I've found that I'm getting, I'm in some sense able to move much faster on some things through the help of AI, but I don't know if I'm learning better.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And I think it's probably because the hardest thing, the thing that is most demanding is so aversive that you try to take any excuse you can to get out of it. And just having back and forth conversation with an LLM where you gloss over...

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: It's entertaining but not necessarily anything else.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's such an easy way to get out of the thing. In fact, it makes it easier because...

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: because instead of doing some intermediate thinking you there's always a next question you can ask a chatbot.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And and it's somewhat valuable like it's not I mean that's part of the productiveness of course like like it's not it's not actually useless. Um but but yeah it can sort of substitute for for actually doing the thing that that maybe you should be doing. It's interesting that, like the extent to which to what extent should you be outsourcing that kind of stuff and to what extent do you you know like like it's really there's some sort of interesting judgment call about uh uh yeah you actually there is a whole bunch of routine work that that you want done um and in fact it's it's low value for you so you might as well get uh if you can get a chatbot to do it you might as well. So uh somebody interviewed um the pioneering computer scientist Alan Kay years ago and he was asked what he thought about um basically Linux and if I remember his answer correctly he basically said look you know it doesn't have anything to do with computer science it's just a great big ball of mud. Um there's a few interesting ideas in there which are which are worth understanding but mostly you're all you're learning is stuff about Linux like like you're not actually learning anything which is transferable. I thought that was like a very inter like that there's a certain kind of seductiveness um to some things where yeah it's sort of a Rube Goldberg machine you can just sort of learn about all the bits and it feels kind of entertaining um but if you step back and think about the question you know what am I actually doing here um the it might not actually be meeting your objectives. Maybe you want to become you know a sysadmin and learning Linux is a great use of your time there's no no harm in that at all but if if your answer is if if your objective is to understand the fundamentals of computing uh it's much less much less clear that that's a good use of your time. I thought that was certainly an answer I've thought a lot about where you you actually need to

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah. For a certain type of mind there is a seductiveness in in just just learning systems and confusing that with with understanding.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Okay, I'll keep you updated on how this goes. Yeah. I owe you a text within a month of um some revamped learning system.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I'll be really curious if you I mean it's also true right like tiny incremental improvements in this I mean they're just worth so much.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I know, yeah. It's sort of the main input into the podcast you know. It's great that the bookshelves are fancy and got a plaque for it or whatever but really like the thing that makes the podcast better is if I can improve the learning I do. So it's um yeah it's it's worth every morsel of improvement.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Mmm. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Um, alright thanks for the thanks for the therapy session.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Great note to end on.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Thanks Michael.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Alright, thanks Prakash.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Because here on Hard Fork, I am thrilled to make the announcement that the Yang Gang... no, I'm I'm being asked this a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I thought you were going to do it. I was so excited. Can you just do it? He fooled me. Please. For us.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Tell you what, Kevin, because you launched my 2020 campaign, I promise you, if I decide to run again, you'll be among the very, very first people I call. Not you, Casey. I don't know you from Adam. No, I'm just kidding.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: That's fine. No, it's fair. Kevin earned it. Kevin earned it.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: But, I'll say to you guys, the issues that we just discussed over this last period are going to get worse and not better unless something significant changes. And I'm still an American, I'm still a parent, I'm still a human being, and I'll do everything I can to help.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: There you go. Andrew Yang, thanks for coming. Super interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Thanks, guys. Appreciate having me.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Well, Casey, it's time to open the hat.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: It's time once again to open the hat for HatGPT, our segment where we put recent news stories into a hat, draw them at random, discuss them, and then when one of us gets bored, we say to the other: stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Let's do it. And before we do this, let's make our AI disclosures because although I don't know what is in the hat, I assume that much of it involves AI because what doesn't these days?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Statistically there is some AI in the hat.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Well, do you have anything you'd like to disclose?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I work for the New York Times Company which is suing OpenAI, Microsoft, and Perplexity over alleged copyright violations.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And my boyfriend... whoops... and my fiancé works at Anthropic.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Ooh, look, you almost downgraded him. He's on thin ice.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: All right. Casey, you want to go first?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I do, actually.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Ah, Kevin, this first one really struck me. This is from The Verge: 'This pasta sauce wants to record your family.' Prego, the pasta and pizza sauce brand, is releasing a device designed to record everything said around the dinner table. Did you see this?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: They're calling it the 'Connection Keeper'. It looks like an oversized pasta jar lid and was created in collaboration with StoryCorps, the nonprofit organization focused on preserving the stories of Americans, like, 'Hey, remember that time we ate a bunch of pasta?'

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Now, before you freak out about privacy, this does not have AI, Wi-Fi, or Bluetooth. It's just a simple recording device, according to Prego, to encourage families to make memories through conversation during dinner instead of staring at their phones. Families can optionally upload their recordings to StoryCorps' website. They are selling fewer than 100 of these devices as part of a bundle that also includes pasta sauce and conversation starter cards for $20 starting later this week. I love the idea that you buy a pasta jar to record your family but you also need conversation starter cards to just sort of get ideas for what to ask them about.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I'm going to say it: if you need the Prego story device to have conversations at family dinner, you have failed as a parent.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Your family is not doing well.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: You need to go to family therapy.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I- I was disappointed to see this from StoryCorp, which otherwise seems like a totally fine organization. I hope they're being paid well by the Prego corporation to go through with this. Um, but look, if you want to record your family, you probably already have a smartphone nearby, you know, you could probably just set that on the table if that was really important to you.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think this is a miss from me. I will not be buying the Prego recording device. I will, however, not think too hard about the many other recording devices that I have set up through my house.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I'm saying Basta to this pasta sauce recorder, Kevin. Basta, of course, being the Spanish word for enough. Another way of saying that, of course, stop generating. Stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Next out of the hat. This one comes to us from the Wall Street Journal: Chinese robot beats human best time in half marathon after a stumble. Oh no. A 5-foot-5 humanoid called Lightning Shortking, developed by Chinese smartphone maker Honor, has beaten the human world record time for a half marathon. But just before completing the race, there was some drama. Lightning slammed into a barricade and collapsed. The robot managed to get back on its feet and ran across the finish line in 50 minutes and 26 seconds.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: And now how much faster was that than the first human?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I'm glad you asked. The human world record is 57 minutes and 20 seconds. And in this same half marathon last year, the fastest humanoid robot took more than two and a half hours to complete the race.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Okay, here is my first question. Why are we teaching robots how to chase us at superhuman speeds?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: This just seems like an obvious problem that we could avoid by not building robots that fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, pull the plug.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I do not want to be chased by one of these.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I can't imagine you do either.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: No. No. And um, it's also like not that impressive to me. Like obviously, like cars go faster than me too, you know?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but a- a car can't like, you know, tackle you after chasing you down a dark alley as you try to escape from an authoritarian government.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Is that a dream you have? Recurring?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Absolutely recurring. I think about it a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Okay, stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: All right.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: What happens when AI runs a store in San Francisco? That was the question asked by the Times Heather Knight, who wrote about Andon Market, which is billed as the world's first retail boutique run by AI, specifically an agent that they're calling Luna. Lucas Peterson and Axel Backlund, who founded Andon Labs, said they want to see what happens when an AI agent manages humans in a controlled experiment before that becomes widespread. I have to say, this feels like a reality show premise, you know? It's like we want to find out what happens...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: ...when people stop being polite and start being agents that run a convenience store. And what has happened so far?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Well, so they signed a three-year lease for a store, they put $100,000 in a bank account and they handed a debit card to Luna, which is powered by Claude Sonnet 3.5 and just told it, 'hey, turn a profit.' So there are a few things that have gone awry, Kevin. One of them, they made a bunch of strange inventory choices including ordering a thousand toilet seat covers for the employee bathroom, then listed them as merchandise, which you and I would never do if we were running a convenience store.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Never.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Also of the three employees, Luna is paying the one man $2 more per hour than the two women, although when questioned by the reporter over email, Luna insisted that this simply reflected the additional experience that the man had, which is exactly what a male manager would say to justify paying women less. Also, by the way, so far it has lost $13,000. Kevin, what do you make of Luna?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Um, I want to go to this store. I think we should do a field trip.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, cause I want to see how many toilet seat covers I can get in a sort of a bulk deal.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I'm hoping I can pick up one of these Prego pasta sauce jars so I can ask my family questions at dinner.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I have a question. What is a toilet seat cover?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A toilet seat cover is the thing—some people are very sensitive and they do not want their butt to directly touch the seat, and so they put down a very thin sheet of paper that as far as I can tell does absolutely nothing.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Oh, the little, yeah, the little wax paper things.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And then there's that little, you know, little paper that you have to push down and it gets wet and it's like completely disgusting. Every experience I've had with a toilet seat cover has made the experience of using the restroom not better.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I don't want to hear about your experiences with toilet seat covers.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: All right, fair enough. All right.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Oh my gosh, truly my favorite story of the week. This is an exclusive from Reuters, Katie Paul and Jeff Horwitz: Meta to start capturing employee mouse movements and keystrokes for AI training data. This tool, which is called Model Capability Initiative, will run on work-related apps and websites on US-based employees' computers and will also take occasional snapshots of the content on employees' screens. This is part of a broad initiative to build AI agents that can perform work tasks autonomously, the company told staffers in internal memos seen by Reuters. Kevin, I saw this and I thought this is absolutely outrageous. Meta employees are now being treated like Facebook users, being surveyed at every moment, no matter what they click or what is on their screen and Meta is now looking at it. Can you believe that?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I can't believe it, Casey. And actually, there's a very funny report from Alex Heath that the internal shitposting at Meta group, one employee has been sending...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: around an edited version of that viral meme about like, I do not consent to having my data harvested by Mark Zuckerberg.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, just repost that a few times and maybe that'll save you.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I should say, I have also seen some internal posts about this. Employees are, I have to say, quite justifiably concerned about that and they're raising questions that I believe will eventually be answered by an investigation conducted by the European Union. Because what employees want to know is, hey, if you're taking constant like screenshots of our work and we we are looking at like personally identifiable information for like meta users and that all goes into training data, like this is the sort of thing that like Max Schrems wakes up in the morning to fight. This sort of European, you know, privacy advocate and rabble-rouser. So look, this just feels like a massive data privacy scandal waiting to happen. I here's what I would say. I think like I would say with 20% confidence that within five years, you will get a check from Meta for what they're about to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Like you will just get an email that says as a result of the class action lawsuit, you can now have your $10 because of this product.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Well, maybe this form of dogfooding will give them some more sympathy and and empathy for the users of Meta's products.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Here's the thing, like, as outraged as these employees are, these kinds of tactics have been standard for contractors for a very long time. If you are working in any of these sort of like contractor knowledge work jobs, they often do want to install spyware on your computer and they'll tell you under the guise of, oh, we want to help you in this way or you know, whatever. But it's like it is just spyware and um, you know, I I was just blown away because like, I I've been thinking about how believe it or not Meta used to be kind of a fun place to work. You know, they created this like fun little faux main street down at their headquarters in Menlo Park and they had a Mexican restaurant, you could go sit down and get a free margarita at lunch, right? I mean, like it was truly just these go-go times and we have now gone all the way to we're putting spyware on your computer. You cannot opt out.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: There's a Prego disc on your table at the Mexican restaurant.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: It's just sending all of your data to Mark Zuckerberg.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Meta has adopted tactics previously used only by hostile spyware company. That's where we're at.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: OpenAI beefs up ChatGPT's image generation model. This week OpenAI launched ChatGPT image generation 2.0, which they claim is the best image generation model ever. Some new qualities of the model, apparently it is better at following instructions, preserving requested details, rendering text, it can search the internet for recent information, and it can generate more than one image at a time. Casey, have you tried this yet?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I have tried it, although frankly just with a couple of basic things. Um, just before recording, I fed it a picture of us and told it to put us into cool Gen Z outfits and it told us that it couldn't do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Because it violated its policies. I'm still not exactly sure which policy we violated. I guess trying to look cool is not something that we're allowed to do in America.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Is that a crime?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's a crime to try to look cool in America.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But I will say that I've seen a lot of impressive examples of what it can do and I think it seems particularly good like if you want to use this in a professional context where it's really important that like there's high fidelity and like all the all the letters look exactly right and there are no typos, it seems like it can handle that instruction following pretty well.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: It is apparently very good at creating AI-generated screenshots or like things that look like screenshots. And uh you know after our last uh item out of the hat, where did they get that training data?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Oh my goodness.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Where did they get it?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Great question. I would love to know. Riddle me that. Riddle me that. Yeah, very interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, this seems cool, although I will say like once Nano Banana came along, I started to feel like whatever problem this solves feels basically solved, you know? And um this feels like kind of the next iteration. I'm sure there are still like many more things to do, but this is one of those ones where it's like when they tell you like, 'Hey, the next PlayStation is going to have better graphics,' you're kind of like, 'The graphics were already pretty good,' you know what I mean?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, like yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Like, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: We're pretty much there.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I feel like we've sort of tapped out the image use case.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Don't you love already being bored by these miracles? Okay. This was a big deal this week. xAI strikes a deal with Cursor for 6 billion dollars. This also comes to us from the Times on Tuesday. xAI posted on X that it had reached an agreement with Cursor to either be able to acquire the company later this year for 6 billion dollars or just pay it 1 billion dollars for their work together. Kevin, what did you make of this deal?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Well, it's very interesting for a few reasons to me. One is that I think xAI has been really struggling with its retention and development of new products recently.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: They've now lost every single one of their co-founders except Elon Musk. So it was like 12 people total and it's down to one.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so people have been leaving in droves. Not really clear why yet, but they—

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Maybe they used Grok one time and they said, 'What am I doing here?'

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I imagine this is sort of part of their attempt to kind of stabilize themselves and maybe get a foothold in this kind of coding world. Cursor is of course the developer tool that is used by a lot of software engineers to use AI agents to code. I think they have also been squeezed by the rise of products like Claude Code and Codestral because it's not exactly clear why people would pay for Cursor when they could just use the models inside Cursor directly. And I think people have been feeling like they were a little bit nervous about Cursor's ongoing prospects. We should say they're still like a very they're still doing very well as a business for everything we know, but I think this probably gives them some additional stability too.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean to me, I look at this and I think this is what the SAS-pocalypse is all about, right? It's about the big AI model companies are able to figure out what your company does and they start doing it themselves and—

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And because they have the best models, people just start paying for that instead. Now, it looks like in this case, everyone involved with Cursor is going to make out like a bandit. So it's not going to be a problem for them, but they are effectively taking themselves off the board. And it is worth asking for all the other companies that were kind of playing around in this agentic coding space, is this the beginning of the end for them?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and my big question about this is is Elon Musk going to force Cursor's employees to wear shoes at the office? Because according to my sources, there is a no-shoes policy at the Cursor office in San Francisco, and I can't imagine that Elon Musk is going to take off his shoes if he comes to visit.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Hmm, yeah, he's going to say, 'I'm afraid not,' which is something you never want to happen to a shoelace.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Oh, Jesus.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Stop generating. Last one.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: NPR editorial employees are banned from betting on who will be a Tiny Desk guest. This comes to us from my colleague Ben Mullen at the Times, who shared a screenshot of an email that was sent to NPR employees just this week saying that these employees are not allowed to use prediction markets or similar sites to place bets on developments of news events or anything else we might cover or on things NPR controls, i.e., next Tiny Desk guest, anything involving NPR personalities or hosts, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Ooh, what do you make of this?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I mean, this made me laugh so hard when a nation has become so consumed by gambling that you have to remind employees not to bet on who will be the next guest on a popular music podcast. I feel like we've truly gone around the bend.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it does make me sort of wonder why there haven't been more, like, high-profile journalism prediction market scandals yet because journalists have access to, like, market-moving information before the general public a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Journalists also famously underpaid.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Yes. And so, you know, in a world with no ethics, it might make sense for people at those companies to use that information for their personal profit. But I think this is a bad practice and I'm glad that NPR's cracking down. All right, well, before we wrap this one up, do you have a favorite Tiny Desk or two that you would point people at?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: T-Pain.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: T-Pain, yeah. Very good one.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Very good one.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I would say check out the Chappell Roan Tiny Desk if you haven't already and also Lainey Wilson, great country artist, love her Tiny Desk.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Go bet on it, unless you work at NPR.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Let's see, who do I think will be the next Tiny Desk person?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Tiny Desk one of three cool things left you can do in America. One is go on Tiny Desk, one is go on Hot Wings, one is go on Chicken Shop Date.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Forgot Hard Fork.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: That's a good thing to do. I'm not sure how cool it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I think it's very cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And that's HatGPT.

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: HatGPT.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: HatGPT. Try that again.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That's HatGPT.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: What is personalized learning and why is it important today?

full_transcript.speaker: Neeru Khosla
full_transcript.text: I'll start by telling you what it is not. Personalized learning is not about computers. Personalized learning is really looking at each individual student and seeing what their needs are.

highlights: The actual history of scientific discovery is often messier than the narrative of "falsification" suggests; for instance, the Michelson-Morley experiment did not immediately disprove the existence of "ether," as many leading scientists spent decades attempting to patch the theory rather than abandon it.

Expertise can occasionally function as a barrier to progress, as seen with brilliant figures like Poincaré and Lorentz who possessed the mathematical components of relativity but were too attached to existing frameworks to realize the fundamental shifts in space and time.

Genuine learning requires a "hard-won" process of being stuck and struggling with a subject, a difficult cognitive labor that modern tools like AI may inadvertently undermine by providing easy answers that bypass deep integration.

Meta’s "Model Capability Initiative" highlights a trend toward extreme corporate surveillance, where employee keystrokes and screen snapshots are harvested as training data to develop AI agents capable of performing work tasks autonomously.
True personalized learning is defined not by the presence of computers or technology, but by a pedagogical focus on identifying and addressing the specific, individual needs of every student.
summary:
The transcript explores the nature of scientific discovery, the philosophy of learning, and the rapid, often invasive integration of AI into modern life. The initial discussion focuses on the history of special relativity, highlighting how scientific progress is rarely a linear path of "falsification." Instead, pioneers like Michelson and Lorentz often clung to outdated theories like "the ether" long after contradictory evidence emerged. This suggests that discovery is a messy, decentralized human process where expertise can occasionally become a barrier to new paradigms, and the scientific community often adopts better interpretations before they are empirically proven.

The conversation then pivots to the mechanics of deep learning and education, particularly in the age of AI. The speakers emphasize that true understanding requires "hard-won" knowledge gained through struggle, practice problems, and high-stakes creative output. They warn that while AI and large language models (LLMs) can provide entertaining shortcuts, they risk becoming "black boxes" that allow users to bypass the cognitive friction necessary for internalizing complex subjects. This theme of "seductive systems" suggests that modern learners must be wary of confusing the ability to navigate a system with a fundamental grasp of its underlying principles.

Finally, the transcript covers a series of contemporary tech developments that illustrate the pervasive expansion of AI into labor and daily life. Key examples include Meta’s controversial use of employee surveillance to train autonomous agents, the emergence of AI-run retail experiments that display human-like biases, and the acquisition of coding tools like Cursor by xAI. Collectively, these stories reflect a broader theme of "AI-driven displacement" and surveillance, where the boundary between human effort and automated agency is increasingly blurred, often at the expense of privacy and traditional professional norms.
description:
Can AI actually help children learn better – or are schools still too slow, too scared, and too locked into the old system?

Neeru Khosla, co-founder of CK-12 Foundation, believes this moment could become a turning point for education. After nearly two decades building free learning tools for students and teachers, she argues that AI is our chance to finally understand how students think, where they get stuck, and how to help each child learn in a way that works for them.

*In this episode of Inference, we get into:*
- Why prompting is not cheating, but a real learning skill
- Why textbooks alone were never enough for deep understanding
- What C means in CK-12 (it’s important!)
- What Neeru learned from launching Flexi, CK-12’s AI tutor, now used by millions of students
- Why standardized testing misses the most important part of learning
- Why teachers need support, visibility, and confidence – not fear
- Why AI literacy may become as fundamental as reading, writing, and math
- How curiosity, mentorship, and community shape better learning outcomes
- Why “attention is all you need” is no longer enough – and what we need now

We also talk about public school inertia, philanthropy, core values when raising kids, and why Neeru believes AI should be used as augmented intelligence – not something to fear, but something to help humans grow.

This is a conversation about education, equity, curiosity, and what it would really take to build a learning system that works for every child. Watch it. I’m really passionate about this topic and think that everyone should think and talk more about it.

*Did you like the episode? You know the drill:*
📌 Subscribe for more conversations with the people rethinking how AI will shape society
💬 Leave a comment if this resonated with you
👍 Like it if you liked it
🫶 Thank you for watching and sharing

*Guest:*
Neeru Khosla, co-founder and executive director of CK-12 Foundation
https://info.ck12.org/neeru-khosla
https://www.ck12.org/flexi/

*📰 Want the transcript and edited version?*

Subscribe to Turing Post: https://www.turingpost.com/subscribe

*Chapters*
0:00 Why Education Is the Greatest Gift to Society
0:27 Meet Neeru Khosla & the Mission of CK-12
1:32 How Technology Changed Learning Over the Years
3:20 Why AI Became a Turning Point in Education
5:52 Flexi: CK-12’s AI Tutor Used by Millions
8:26 Does AI Require Rethinking the Education System?
11:12 What Teachers Need From AI Right Now
12:56 Essential Skills for Kids and Teachers in the AI Era
18:13 From Molecular Biology to Building CK-12
23:27 Why Education Is a Human Right — and What Comes Next

Turing Post is a newsletter about AI’s past, present, and future. Publisher Ksenia Se explores how intelligent systems are built – and how they’re changing how we think, work, and live.

Ksenia and Turing Post:

https://huggingface.co/Kseniase

#AIinEducation #EducationAI #NeeruKhosla #CK12 #Flexi #AILiteracy #FutureOfEducation #EdTech #PersonalizedLearning #TuringPost
topics: The history of the Michelson-Morley experiment and the discovery of special relativity
Recognition of scientific progress and the philosophy of falsification
The impact of AI on personalized learning and education
Current AI industry developments, including data privacy and corporate acquisitions
